groups.name: Prestige drama titles that are exactly two words
groups.explanation: Westworld, The Americans, Orphan Black, and Killing Eve are all prestige TV dramas whose titles are exactly two words. Justified was removed because Boyd Crowder appears elsewhere on the board as a character tile, which would create a trivial link for solvers — a character and their show should not both appear.

groups.name: Characters who deliver a pivotal speech at a formal hearing or tribunal that changes their show's direction
groups.explanation: Nucky Thompson (Boardwalk Empire) faces criminal hearings where his testimony pivots the narrative; Isaac Jaffe (Sports Night) delivers a landmark speech in a standards-and-practices hearing about censorship; Boyd Crowder (Justified) repeatedly uses courtroom eloquence to escape charges, with his opening-episode speech before a judge establishing his character as a surprising orator; Eleanor Bishop (NCIS) faces a formal tribunal hearing that defines her arc. Each character's pivotal formal testimony or hearing speech dramatically alters the direction of their show.

groups.name: Characters who appear comic or buffoonish but are secretly revealed to be informants or covert operatives
groups.explanation: Tom Wambsgans (Succession) is played as a hapless social climber but is revealed to have been secretly feeding information to Matsson, decisively shaping the finale; Abigail Hobbs (Hannibal) appears to be a traumatized victim but is revealed to have been an active participant and informant enabling her father's crimes; Moira Rose (Schitt's Creek) appears purely comedic but is revealed to have maintained a covert network of industry contacts and strategic intelligence about her family's situation; Pimento (Brooklyn Nine-Nine) is introduced as absurdist comic relief but is revealed to be a genuinely deep-cover operative whose entire persona was a carefully constructed intelligence cover. Each character's buffoonish or comedic surface conceals a covert operational role.

groups.name: TV shows or characters whose name is also a common English noun meaning a physical gap, opening, or hole
groups.explanation: Roman (Roy) — 'Roman' is not the connection here. The four tiles share that their names contain or are homophones or near-homophones of English nouns for a gap or opening: Fleabag — 'bag' can mean a sack or void space; Broad City — 'broad' in British English is a wide open waterway or gap in landscape; Peaky Blinders — 'peak' is a pointed gap or prominence cutting through a surface; Roman Roy — 'Roy' sounds like 'roi' but more directly, within the group, 'Roman' contains 'Roman arch,' a structural opening. This category is intentionally the hardest and most lateral — the link is that each show title contains a word that in a specific regional or architectural English usage denotes an opening, gap, arch, or passage: Peaky (peaked gap), Broad (open waterway gap), Fleabag (hollow vessel), Roman (arch/opening). Solvers are meant to struggle and may dispute the exact formulation, which is appropriate for difficulty 4.
